data.title: Man arrested as Hong Kong police seize illegal fireworks worth HK$1 million
data.description:
Hong Kong police have charged a man with illegal possession of fireworks and firecrackers after seizing a haul worth HK$1 million (US$128,200) from a shipping container in Tuen Mun, three weeks ahead of the Lunar New Year.
Officers from the New Territories North regional anti-triad unit found 58 boxes containing 800kg (1,764lbs) of pyrotechnics during a raid in Tai Lam Chung at about 4pm on Monday, the force said.
Senior Inspector Kwok Man-wai said police found the goods in a container rented by the 37-year-old suspect, adding that he was scheduled to appear at Fanling Court on February 12.
ŌĆ£Police were committed to an intelligence-led operation to combat fireworks- and firecracker-related offences, intercepting the goods before they flowed into the market,ŌĆØ Kwok said.
ŌĆ£These items are extremely likely to cause fires or other serious accidents, posing a major threat to personal and public safety.ŌĆØ
Those convicted of illegal possession of fireworks or firecrackers under the Dangerous Goods Ordinance face a maximum penalty of 12 monthsŌĆÖ imprisonment and a HK$200,000 fine.
Under the Summary Offences Ordinance, anyone who sets off fireworks that endanger people or cause damage to property can be fined HK$2,000 and imprisoned for up to three months.
ŌĆ£Police would like to take this opportunity to appeal to the public again during the festive celebration period not to purchase or use illegal fireworks and firecrackers,ŌĆØ Kwok said.

data.title: ChinaŌĆÖs Alibaba, Moonshot claim latest AI models challenge OpenAI, Google
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding and Moonshot AI have unveiled their latest flagship artificial intelligence models, narrowing the gap with US industry leaders OpenAI and Google DeepMind.
The new models from two of ChinaŌĆÖs leading AI developers mark the first major Chinese model releases of this year, as industry observers continue to closely monitor the gap between the US and China in developing cutting-edge foundational models.
Alibaba Cloud announced its biggest-ever model, Qwen3-Max-Thinking, on Monday, which it said boasted stronger agentic and tool use capabilities. The model was released on AlibabaŌĆÖs official cloud-services platform and chatbot website, with the model set to be available on its new consumer Qwen app ŌĆ£soonŌĆØ, it said.
Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing unit of Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the Post.
ŌĆ£This is our best model so far,ŌĆØ said Zheng Chujie, an Alibaba researcher, on social media. ŌĆ£We have ŌĆ” put massive efforts [into] improving real-world user experience.ŌĆØ
The model is the latest in the Hangzhou-based companyŌĆÖs Qwen3 series, first released in May, which initially consisted of models ranging from 600 million to 235 billion parameters and has since reached models of more than 1 trillion parameters, including Qwen3-Max-Thinking.

Parameters are the variables encoding an AI modelŌĆÖs intelligence, which are adjusted during training. In general, a higher number of parameters means a more capable model, although more computational resources are also needed.
In a blog post, Alibaba said that the new modelŌĆÖs performance stemmed from a further increase in model size and ŌĆ£substantial computational resources for reinforcement learningŌĆØ.
Alibaba said it used new techniques to improve the efficiency of ŌĆ£test-time scalingŌĆØ ŌĆō adding more computational resources during model deployment ŌĆō by focusing more computational resources on unresolved uncertainties, thereby boosting performance without significant computational overhead.
On internal tests, Alibaba said the model achieved ŌĆ£comparableŌĆØ performance to leading US models such as AnthropicŌĆÖs Claude-Opus-4.5 and Google DeepMindŌĆÖs Gemini 3 Pro across 19 benchmarks.
However, some Qwen fans expressed disappointment that the model had not been open-sourced. While Qwen is one of the worldŌĆÖs most popular open-source model families, Alibaba has kept its biggest and most powerful Max models closed-source so far and used them to power its Qwen app.
Meanwhile, Alibaba-backed start-up Moonshot AI announced its newest model Kimi K2.5 on Tuesday, calling it the worldŌĆÖs most powerful open-source model.
Notably, the new model introduces a feature called agent swarm, allowing developers to better tackle complex coding tasks by using up to 100 subagents that execute workflows in parallel.
The company said the feature would greatly benefit programmers using Kimi models, which the Beijing-based start-up has pitched as cheaper alternatives to industry leader AnthropicŌĆÖs Claude models in the key AI application market of coding.
The new model marks the first time Moonshot has added multimodal capabilities to its products, enabling processing and generation of video and images in addition to text.

Both Moonshot and the Qwen team have been proponents of scaling laws in AI ŌĆō the theory that adding computational power and data will deliver reliable gains in model performance.
In a road map unveiled in September, the Qwen team announced the goal of scaling up its models to more than 10 trillion parameters to keep pace with US counterparts that were already training models approaching that size.
However, Qwen team leader Lin Junyang hinted at an industry event earlier this month that computational constraints posed a major challenge, as resources were being tied up in daily delivery instead of being used for research and development.

data.title: Hong Kong to deny teachers practising certificates with insufficient training hours
data.description:
Teachers who fail to complete the required hours of professional development will be denied practising certificates, Hong KongŌĆÖs education authorities have said, ahead of meeting next week to discuss details of the proposed scheme.
Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin said the proposal would also outline the requirements for rejoining the profession after registered teachers had left the sector for a period of time.
The proposed certificate was introduced as a new measure in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiuŌĆÖs policy address in September. Under the plan, teachers will be required to obtain the certification from the Education Bureau ŌĆō valid for a set period and subject to regular renewal ŌĆō in addition to their existing teacher registration.
Choi told the Legislative CouncilŌĆÖs education panel on Tuesday that the government would discuss the proposed certificate next week, with the meeting scheduled for February 6.
The panel will also address the Code of Practice for Private Schools, following reports that some local institutions had partnered with mainland Chinese agencies to offer classes. The government subsequently revoked or suspended the registrations of at least two schools.
Choi said the introduction of the certificate requiring regular renewal aimed to ensure all teachers were ŌĆ£fit and properŌĆØ, thereby safeguarding professional teaching standards.

ŌĆ£Our database shows that out of 160,000 registered teachers, only about 70,000 to 80,000 are currently practising. Some hold teaching licences but chose not to enter the profession for various reasons,ŌĆØ she said.
ŌĆ£We will also discuss how to address the situation of teachers rejoining the sector next week.ŌĆØ
Last September, Choi said authorities would consider a range of factors before deciding whether to grant the certificate, including whether applicants live in Hong Kong.
Deputy Secretary of the Education Bureau Verena Lau Wing-yin said the professional development requirement involved completing at least 150 hours of training over three years, including 30 hours of core training. She noted that most teachers had already met this requirement.
She said the 150-hour threshold would serve as a baseline for determining the amount of core training required.
According to the bureauŌĆÖs website, the core training covers teachersŌĆÖ professional roles, values and conduct, as well as local, national and international education issues.
ŌĆ£If teachers cannot fulfil the teachersŌĆÖ training requirement, they will temporarily be unable to obtain the practising certificate,ŌĆØ Lau said.

data.title: MTR Corp raises record A$2 billion in first Australian dollar green bond sale
data.description:
Hong KongŌĆÖs rail giant has sold its first-ever Australian dollar-denominated green bonds for a record A$2 billion (US$1.4 billion) to fund environmentally friendly projects, marking the countryŌĆÖs largest order book.
The bonds, issued in two equal tranches, were oversubscribed more than sixfold by a variety of investors, the MTR Corporation said on Tuesday.
The offering represented the largest-ever Australian dollar corporate green bond and the largest dual-tranche corporate bond in the market. It was also the biggest corporate order book ever achieved in the countryŌĆÖs currency.
ŌĆ£The issuance of our inaugural Australian dollar green bonds is the next step in our efforts to maintain a highly diversified funding base to drive business growth,ŌĆØ MTR finance director Michael Fitzgerald said.
ŌĆ£We are delighted with the very positive response from investors, including insurance companies, official institutions, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, asset managers, banks, securities houses and private banks from around the world.ŌĆØ
He added the proceeds would further strengthen the corporationŌĆÖs financial foundation and enhance its flexibility to support the capital investment programme, which will enable it to build more vibrant communities and uphold service excellence.
An MTR spokesman said the bonds were issued in a dual-tranche transaction comprising a five-year tranche of A$1 billion and a 12-year tranche of A$1 billion, with coupon rates set at 4.886 per cent and 5.582 per cent, respectively.

He added that strong demand led to an oversubscription of 6.25 times, with a combined final order book of A$12.5 billion, and that the positive market response underscored investor confidence in the corporationŌĆÖs commitment to sustainability.
ANZ, Deutsche Bank, MUFG and UBS acted as joint lead managers and joint bookrunners for the debt issuance, with ANZ appointed as the coordinator for environmental, social and governance (ESG) initiatives.
The MTR Corp has had a presence in Australia since 2009 and currently operates major rail franchises and metro systems in Melbourne and Sydney.
Last year, MTR secured a landmark HK$30 billion (US$3.86 billion) seven-year syndicated green term loan with the support of 57 banks worldwide in September, as well as a public bond issue that raised US$3 billion from a three-tranche offering amid a hot credit market in Asia in March.
In July, the rail giant and the Hong Kong government signed an agreement on the design and construction of a major rail link in the Northern Metropolis, fast-tracking its completion by at least two years to 2034.
The agreement, which gives fresh momentum to the Northern Metropolis project, involves HK$39.05 billion in financing from land premiums for the construction of three stations along the Northern Link main line, as well as the design of the spur line, tunnels and other railway facilities. It is the first part of a two-stage deal.
First announced in the 2021 policy address, the Northern Metropolis development aims to transform 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) in the New Territories into an economic powerhouse featuring a new IT hub and a university town with high-quality living spaces.

data.title: ChinaŌĆÖs Baidu merges key app businesses to drive consumer AI push
data.description:
Baidu has merged two of its consumer app businesses into a new artificial intelligence-focused unit, stepping up its push into ChinaŌĆÖs fast-evolving and fiercely contested market for consumer AI applications.
The company combined its online document-sharing business Wenku and consumer-oriented cloud storage operation Wangpan to form the Personal Super Intelligence Business Group (PSIG), according to a source familiar with the matter. The move marks one of BaiduŌĆÖs largest reorganisations in the past two years.
PSIG would be headed by Wang Ying, a company vice-president who oversaw the two separate businesses prior to the change, the source added. The set-up was first reported by local business and financial outlet Caijing, citing sources who said Wang would report directly to Robin Li Yanhong, company founder and CEO.
Baidu did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The move comes amid intensifying competition in ChinaŌĆÖs consumer AI sector, as Big Tech and AI specialist firms race to turn their services and apps into the next ŌĆ£super appŌĆØ.

The restructuring highlighted the new unitŌĆÖs strategic role in BaiduŌĆÖs AI commercialisation push, as its traditional advertising revenue declined and users increasingly turned to chatbot apps to conduct searches, according to Zhang Yi, founder and chief analyst at consultancy iiMedia.
Zhang said embedding AI more deeply into services such as Wenku and Wangpan ŌĆō both of which charge premium features ŌĆō could lift their paying user base. A larger customer pool would also generate more data to refine BaiduŌĆÖs personalised search and content-generation tools.
Wenku and Wangpan rank among BaiduŌĆÖs most popular consumer-facing services, with both undergoing AI upgrades in recent years to add capabilities from generating charts and slide decks to drafting reports from simple prompts.
Wenku now hosted more than 1.8 billion documents across a wide range of industries and topics and had 97 million monthly users, while Wangpan had more than 200 million monthly users, including 80 million who used its AI additions, according to data from the company.
BaiduŌĆÖs doubling down on consumer AI applications comes as other Big Tech firms shift focus from competing on model performance to embedding AI into their businesses.
Earlier this month, Alibaba Group Holding announced a major update to its flagship Qwen AI app, connecting it to its wider portfolio of services. Alibaba owns the Post.
Companies are also ramping up promotion of their AI apps amid intensifying competition. Tencent said on Sunday it would spend 1 billion yuan ($143 million) on its Lunar New Year campaign, while Baidu had set aside 500 million yuan, as both sought to leverage the holiday to draw in new users to their respective AI chatbot apps, Yuanbao and Wenxin.

data.title: AlibabaŌĆÖs Qwen-3 becomes worldŌĆÖs first AI model to operate in orbit
data.description:
Alibaba CloudŌĆÖs Qwen-3 became the worldŌĆÖs first general-purpose artificial intelligence model to be uploaded and operated in orbit, marking a milestone in ChinaŌĆÖs bid to lead the nascent space-based computing sector.
Chinese aerospace start-up Adaspace Technology successfully deployed Qwen-3 to a space computing centre in orbit where it executed multiple inference tasks in November, according to company executive vice-president Wang Yabo, who spoke at a conference on Monday, as reported by the Star Market Daily.
Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing services unit of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
Wang said Qwen-3 was deployed in AdaspaceŌĆÖs initial space computing centre that formed part of the firmŌĆÖs ŌĆ£Star-Compute ProjectŌĆØ, a 2,800-satellite network designed to power physical AI and support AI model training and inference.
The entire Qwen-3 process ŌĆō from uploading queries from the ground to performing on-orbit inference and sending results back to Earth ŌĆō was completed in less than two minutes, according to Wang.
Qwen-3, released in April last year, strengthened the position of Alibaba CloudŌĆÖs Qwen family as the worldŌĆÖs largest open-source AI ecosystem, surpassing Meta PlatformsŌĆÖ Llama community.
AdaspaceŌĆÖs initial space computing centre, which was launched into space in May last year, represented the worldŌĆÖs first AI computing satellite constellation, comprising 12 satellites, according to a Securities Times report.
The companyŌĆÖs Qwen-3 operation underscored ChinaŌĆÖs big head start in orbital data processing, which takes advantage of the unlimited solar energy and lower cooling demands in space, falling launch costs and reduced data transmission costs, while bypassing the physical bottlenecks of ground-based computing.

The second and third space computing centres from Adaspace were currently in production and scheduled for orbital deployment in 2026, according to a report by Jiemian News.
The entire Star-Compute Project will include 2,400 inference satellites and 400 training units deployed in low-earth orbit, with full deployment expected to be completed by 2035.
Founded in 2018, Adaspace had applied for an initial public offering on the Hong Kong stock exchange. Headquartered in Chengdu, the capital of southwestern Sichuan province, the company was valued at over 6.76 billion yuan (US$972 million) after its latest financing round in July last year, according to a Securities Times report.
AdaspaceŌĆÖs business model covered the entire satellite life cycle, from design, research and development, manufacturing and launch coordination to in-orbit operations and satellite-based solutions.
The company had completed 14 space missions to date, including the development and launch of 33 satellites and payloads, according to Jiemian News.
China has designated aerospace as a strategic priority in its 15th five-year plan through 2030. Fuelled by BeijingŌĆÖs policy support, the domestic satellite industry was expected to grow steadily and reach 266.1 billion yuan by 2029 from around 82.7 billion yuan in 2024, according to data from the Satellite Industry Association and Frost & Sullivan.
China and the US currently compete head-to-head in orbital launches. In 2025, the US conducted 180 orbital launches, followed by ChinaŌĆÖs 93, according to data from GunterŌĆÖs Space Page.

data.title: On This Day|Hong KongŌĆÖs Cathay Pacific rebrands Dragonair subsidiary in 2016 ŌĆō SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on January 27, 2016
Exit the Dragon┬Łair as Cathay rebrands
by Sijia Jiang
Cathay Pacific group is likely to announce a rebrand┬Łing of its Dragon┬Łair sub┬Łsi┬Łdi┬Łary as ŌĆ£Cathay DragonŌĆØ as the com┬Łpany seeks to strengthen its cor┬Łpor┬Łate iden┬Łtity.
The idea to strengthen the sis┬Łter air┬ŁlineŌĆÖs asso┬Łci┬Łation with the premium air┬Łline Cathay Pacific Air┬Łways has been under dis┬Łcus┬Łsion for a few months, sources famil┬Łiar with the mat┬Łter told the South China Morn┬Łing Post.
CathayŌĆÖs man┬Łager of cor┬Łpor┬Łate com┬Łmu┬Łnic┬Ła┬Łtions, Car┬Ło┬Łlyn Leung, declined to com┬Łment on whether the com┬Łpany had any rebrand┬Łing plans for Dragon┬Łair.
ŌĆ£Fol┬Łlow┬Łing the suc┬Łcess┬Łful launch of Cathay PacificŌĆÖs new brand ethos and refreshed cor┬Łpor┬Łate iden┬Łtity, we have been review┬Łing Dragon┬ŁairŌĆÖs over┬Łall brand pro┬Łpos┬Łi┬Łtion. We will provide more inform┬Ła┬Łtion as appro┬Łpri┬Łate.ŌĆØ

The move would put an end to the Dragon┬Łair name which was foun┬Łded by tex┬Łtile mag┬Łnate Chow Kuang Piu in 1985. The air┬Łline was acquired by Cathay in 2006 fol┬Łlow┬Łing a major share┬Łhold┬Łing realign┬Łment involving CathayŌĆÖs par┬Łent Swire Pacific and the Air China Group. Dragon┬Łair, which has con┬Łtin┬Łued to oper┬Łate as a sep┬Łar┬Łate air┬Łline within the group with its own visual iden┬Łtity fea┬Łtur┬Łing a red dragon, mainly flies to main┬Łland China and else┬Łwhere in Asia. Its fares are cheaper than Cathay. Sixty per cent of its pas┬Łsen┬Łgers hold Hong Kong or main┬Łland Chinese pass┬Łports accord┬Łing to the com┬Łpany.
Will Hor┬Łton, senior ana┬Łlyst with the Centre for Avi┬Łation, said renam┬Łing Dragon┬Łair ŌĆ£Cathay DragonŌĆØ would be a good move at the right time, as the Dragon┬Łair brand has weak pas┬Łsen┬Łger recog┬Łni┬Łtion bey┬Łond Greater China and lacked brand asso┬Łci┬Łation with the lar┬Łger Cathay group.
ŌĆ£What is crit┬Łical is that it is not a full-on mer┬Łger. Main┬Łtain┬Łing a sep┬Łar┬Łate regional unit will allow the group to lever┬Łage the stronger and global Cathay brand, while at the same time main┬Łtain┬Łing Dragon┬ŁairŌĆÖs traffic rights and lower oper┬Łat┬Łing cost.ŌĆØ
The rebrand┬Łing is expec┬Łted to affect the air┬ŁlineŌĆÖs visual iden┬Łtity across the board ŌĆō most not┬Łably the air┬Łcraft liv┬Łery. But it is not expec┬Łted to affect Dragon┬ŁairŌĆÖs hold┬Łing of a sep┬Łar┬Łate air oper┬ŁatorŌĆÖs cer┬Łti┬Łfic┬Łate, which allows the group to max┬Łim┬Łise traffic rights usage.
Hor┬Łton said the move would fol┬Łlow a trend among Asian air┬Łline groups with mul┬Łtiple units start┬Łing to bet┬Łter lever┬Łage their brands.
ŌĆ£This comes as AsiaŌĆÖs air┬Łline fam┬Łil┬Łies start to stitch together their air┬Łlines and become integ┬Łrated groups. AsiaŌĆÖs ŌĆśair┬ŁlineŌĆÖ con┬Łglom┬Łer┬Łates have had many air┬Łline licences that they havenŌĆÖt neces┬Łsar┬Łily used cohes┬Łively.ŌĆØ
Since brand cohe┬Łsion was inev┬Łit┬Łable, it is bet┬Łter to do it earlier than later when more pas┬Łsen┬Łgers have to be re-edu┬Łcated,ŌĆØ he said.
Cathay Pacific Group later confirmed the news. This article was first published on January 29, 2016.
Dragon┬Łair found┬Łing fam┬Łily ŌĆśsadŌĆÖ to see brand change
by Sijia Jiang
The end of three dec┬Łades of the Dragon┬Łair name and the iconic red dragon logo that adorned the planes of Hong KongŌĆÖs first local air┬Łline has stirred fond memor┬Łies for the daugh┬Łter of the car┬ŁrierŌĆÖs founder.
The air┬ŁlineŌĆÖs cur┬Łrent owner, Cathay Pacific Group, announced on January 28, 2016 that Dragon┬Łair will be renamed Cathay Dragon, as repor┬Łted by the South China Morn┬Łing Post on January 26, 2016.
ŌĆ£The name change is news to me. However, under┬Łstand┬Łing how big cor┬Łpor┬Ła┬Łtions work, it does not sur┬Łprise me at all,ŌĆØ Diana Chou, daugh┬Łter of founder Chao Kuang-piu, told the Post.
ŌĆ£If rebrand┬Łing can enhance brand equity value, I am sure share┬Łhold┬Łers will vote for it,ŌĆØ said Chou, who remains influ┬Łen┬Łtial in Hong Kong avi┬Łation circles and runs her own private jet busi┬Łness called LŌĆÖvoy┬Łage.
ŌĆ£Nev┬Łer┬Łthe┬Łless, it is sad that such decision has been made as I have very fond memor┬Łies of how involved my father was in design┬Łing and pick┬Łing the name and logo. Our fam┬Łily exited Dragon┬Łair many years ago. Regard┬Łless of the changes, we will always be remembered as the founders of the com┬Łpany,ŌĆØ she said.
Dragon┬Łair, which became a wholly-owned unit of Cathay in 2006, has kept its brand iden┬Łtity largely unchanged for nearly 31 years since it was foun┬Łded by tex┬Łtile mag┬Łnate Chao in May 1985 to chal┬Łlenge the Swire Group air┬Łline.
Its Chinese-themed liv┬Łery was a stark con┬Łtrast to the green of Cathay. The dragon mark will make way for Cathay PacificŌĆÖs brush┬Łwing design, although the red col┬Łour┬Łing will be retained.
Cathay, together with Citic Pacific and Swire Group, bought 89 per cent of Dragon┬Łair in 1990 before even┬Łtu┬Łally tak┬Łing it over for HK$8.22 bil┬Łlion in 2006.
ŌĆ£It is import┬Łant to note that we are not mer┬Łging the air┬Łlines. Cathay Pacific and Cathay Dragon will con┬Łtinue to be two sep┬Łar┬Łate premium air┬Łlines in the group, both oper┬Łat┬Łing under their own licences,ŌĆØ the groupŌĆÖs chief exec┬Łut┬Łive, Ivan Chu, said in unveil┬Łing the plan. He added that HK$100 mil┬Łlion would be spent to pro┬Łmote the Cathay Dragon brand.
He said the com┬Łpany had weighed the com┬Łmer┬Łcial, legal and reg┬Łu┬Łlat┬Łory impact of dif┬Łfer┬Łent options and con┬Łcluded this was the best way to lever┬Łage the strength of both brands in the grow┬Łing air travel mar┬Łket in and out of China.
ŌĆ£We believe Dragon┬Łair has strong brand equity in China. Cathay Pacific actu┬Łally needs to lever┬Łage the Dragon┬Łair brand in China. By 2020, 200 mil┬Łlion Chinese are expec┬Łted to fly out of China,ŌĆØ he said.
ŌĆ£But also bear in mind, 130 mil┬Łlion are expec┬Łted to fly into China from dif┬Łfer┬Łent parts of the world, so we would like the Cathay brand to lend its strength to Dragon┬Łair,ŌĆØ he added.
He added that it was ŌĆ£out of con┬Łsid┬Łer┬Ła┬ŁtionŌĆØ to make Cathay Dragon a low-cost sub┬Łsi┬Łdi┬Łary.
There will be no changes to the air┬ŁlineŌĆÖs cabin crew uni┬Łforms, and the first seven planes out of its fleet of 41 will be repainted this year, the com┬Łpany said.

data.title: How ChinaŌĆÖs AI video generator Kling challenges GoogleŌĆÖs Veo, OpenAIŌĆÖs Sora
data.description:
As AI video generation has evolved from a curiosity to a productivity tool embraced by serious creators, the Kling platform developed by ChinaŌĆÖs Kuaishou has landed firmly in the top tier alongside GoogleŌĆÖs Veo and OpenAIŌĆÖs Sora.
Kling has rapidly grown into a meaningful new business line for the company ŌĆō long a runner-up to TikTok owner ByteDance in ChinaŌĆÖs short video arena ŌĆō since its launch in June 2024, reaching about 12 million monthly active users and annual recurring revenue of roughly US$240 million.
The figures are hardly surprising at a time when dancing puppies, alien invasions and digital human live streaming hosts are already everyday scroll-past content, and ŌĆ£AI slopŌĆØ has become a common concern. While KlingŌĆÖs success is welcome news for Kuaishou, the question is why Kling has become such a hit.
The toolŌĆÖs latest metrics, from sources and company announcements, show its momentum. It garnered more than US$20 million in revenue in December alone, bringing it to US$140 million for the year, more than double the US$60 million target Kuaishou set internally at the start of 2025. In January, average daily revenue jumped about 30 per cent from a month earlier.
That traction has lifted KuaishouŌĆÖs long-sluggish Hong Kong shares, which gained 23.3 per cent over the past month to close at HK$78.60 on Monday.
Kuaishou positions Kling as the new poster child for the generative-AI era. The Beijing-based company set up a stand-alone business unit in April 2025 for the tool, and executives have repeatedly pointed to its rising user base and revenue in earnings calls over the past year.
JPMorgan Chase, in a January research note, highlighted KlingŌĆÖs monetisation potential, forecasting 1.7 billion yuan (US$244 million) in revenue in 2026 and calling Kuaishou one of the ŌĆ£most undervalued AI stocks globallyŌĆØ.
Leading AI models, strong interactive design and deep engagement with the creator ecosystem were the three factors underpinning the video generatorŌĆÖs breakout, according to Zeng Yushen, head of operations for Kling.
ŌĆ£A very clear signal last year from the industry was the embrace of AI,ŌĆØ Zeng said in an interview earlier this month at the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas. ŌĆ£Meanwhile, real-world use cases have been quickly expanding.ŌĆØ
Kling rolled out seven upgrades to its foundational models last year. These included Kling O1, which the company touts as the industryŌĆÖs first unified multimodal video model, capable of handling images, video, subjects and text within a single prompt, while also allowing on-the-fly editing in post production. Another update, Kling 2.6, significantly improved audio generation.
As rankings across video-generation benchmarks continue to shift, Kling has landed firmly in the top tier alongside GoogleŌĆÖs Veo and OpenAIŌĆÖs Sora.

As a tool, Kling leans hard into ease of use and alleviating user pain points. For example, it integrates DeepSeekŌĆÖs model to help with prompt writing. A feature called motion control, which lets uploaded subjects mirror the movement in a reference video, fuelled viral trends such as dancing puppies and drew new users to the platform. Another function, dubbed Smart Canvas, combines generation and editing in a single workspace and also enables real-time collaboration.
Kling targets professional creators and enterprise customers, who tend to have a higher willingness to pay than average consumers. These users, through tiered memberships and application programming interface access, account for about 70 per cent of total revenue, the company said. Kuashiao plans to push Kling to the broader consumer market later.
Compared with many leading global models, ŌĆ£KlingŌĆÖs taste feels closer to what Chinese creators want,ŌĆØ said Li Brian, a television director at a regional broadcaster who uses AI in tasks from brainstorming to visual-effects production.
KlingŌĆÖs Zeng said professional creators shared a common set of needs, including clarity, controllability and consistency, which shaped KlingŌĆÖs decision to launch globally from day one. About 70 per cent of its revenue now came from overseas markets, with the US accounting for roughly a third, according to Sensor Tower data.
Operationally and in marketing, however, Kling took a more localised approach, closely tracking regional social-media trends, creator communities and production studios, Zeng said.
Li cautioned that his endorsement of Kling was ŌĆ£temporaryŌĆØ. AI tools were evolving so quickly, he said, that industry insiders were shifting from one platform to another, with little loyalty so far.
Zhong Zhong, a Shanghai-based film producer, said she currently preferred Sora 2, released in September, citing its stronger performance in maintaining character consistency during pre-production tasks such as previsualisation and scene testing.
For now, no single player has established an undisputed lead. KlingŌĆÖs Chinese peers, including ByteDanceŌĆÖs Jimeng, AlibabaŌĆÖs Wan and TencentŌĆÖs Hunyuan, as well as start-ups such as MinimaxŌĆÖs Hailuo, PixVerse and ShengshuŌĆÖs Vidu, are all racing to compete.
None of the tools were good enough yet, Zhong said, pointing to persistent weaknesses in shot continuity, the realism of performances and the handling of emotion.
Even so, a consensus is taking hold that AI adoption is inevitable. As AI-generated content floods the market, creators are being pushed to reconsider not just how they will use these tools, but also how to revalue themselves.

data.title: ChinaŌĆÖs Anta snags Puma from FranceŌĆÖs Pinault family in US$1.8 billion deal
data.description:
Anta Sports, ChinaŌĆÖs largest sportswear firm, is acquiring 29 per cent of German brand Puma from Groupe Artemis of the Pinault family, one of FranceŌĆÖs wealthiest, for Ōé¼1.5 billion (US$1.8 billion).
The Fujian-headquartered group said on Tuesday the deal would ŌĆ£accelerateŌĆØ its multi-brand globalisation strategy, adding it had no plans to pursue a full takeover or make an offer for Puma Hong Kong. The deal is expected to be completed by the end of the year.
ŌĆ£This acquisition makes Anta Sports the largest shareholder of Puma and marks a major step forward in our ŌĆśsingle-focus, multi-brand, globalisationŌĆÖ strategy,ŌĆØ said Ding Shizhong, chairman of the group.
ŌĆ£Puma is an iconic global brand with substantial heritage. Working with Puma, we look forward to learning from each other and joining hands to fully unlock the brandŌĆÖs full potential.ŌĆØ
The transaction adds to AntaŌĆÖs string of acquisitions since 2018, including the US$6.4 billion purchase of Finland-based Amer Sports ŌĆō which owns ArcŌĆÖteryx, Salomon and Wilson ŌĆō Chinese athleisure brand Maia Active, and German outdoor label Jack Wolfskin, acquired for US$250 million.

The Pinault family, which controls global luxury group Kering ŌĆō owner of brands like Bottega Veneta, Gucci and Saint Laurent ŌĆō acquired PumaŌĆÖs controlling stake in 2007. Since then it has reduced its stake but remained its single largest shareholder.
AntaŌĆÖs acquisition would be financed entirely with internal cash resources, the group said.
Puma is recognised worldwide for its sports and casual footwear, including Puma Clyde, and for collaborations with global celebrities like BlackpinkŌĆÖs Rose, and pop superstars Rihannna and Dua Lipa, among others. Its key sports markets include Europe, Latin America, Africa and India.
Its product line spans diversified sporting activities such as football, running, training, basketball and motorsport.
ŌĆ£Anta has always admired PumaŌĆÖs long-term brand value and potential,ŌĆØ Ding said. ŌĆ£Such a strong brand DNA and value heritage are rare to come by.ŌĆØ
Moving forward, the group hoped to ŌĆ£build strong trust, work together, and leverage our complementary strengths without compromising independenceŌĆØ, Ding said. ŌĆ£We look forward to supporting the brandŌĆÖs ongoing revival.ŌĆØ
Anta, founded in 1991, has been expanding operations across key markets, including Southeast Asia, the Middle East, Africa, North America and Europe.

data.title: Geely-linked veteran Yin Qi joins Chinese AI start-up StepFun as chairman
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence start-up StepFun has appointed Yin Qi, the co-founder and former CEO of Megvii, as its chairman, as the company wraps up a 5 billion yuan (US$690 million) Series B+ funding round and sharpens its focus on so-called ŌĆ£physical AIŌĆØ ŌĆō artificial intelligence designed to power real-world machines such as cars and robots.
Yin, a prominent figure from ChinaŌĆÖs first wave of computer-vision start-ups, is also chairman of Qianli Technology, a Geely-backed smart-driving firm.
The appointment reinforced StepFunŌĆÖs ŌĆ£AI plus terminal devicesŌĆØ strategy, the company said, leveraging YinŌĆÖs experience in commercialising AI technologies, particularly in smart cars.
Founded in 2023, Shanghai-based StepFun was set up by Jiang Daxin, former chief scientist at Microsoft Research Asia.
The company said its core management team would consist of Yin as chairman, Jiang as CEO, and Zhu Yibo, a former Microsoft and ByteDance AI veteran, as chief technology officer.
Yi would be responsible for setting the companyŌĆÖs overall strategic pace and technological direction, the company said in a statement.
ŌĆ£Qianli is building AI-enabled application scenarios centred on vehicles,ŌĆØ Yin said in a recent podcast. ŌĆ£StepFun, meanwhile, provides the underlying foundation models ŌĆō the ŌĆśbrainŌĆÖ and core AI capabilities that support.ŌĆØ
QianliŌĆÖs Shanghai-listed shares rose about 4.4 per cent on Monday to close at 12 yuan.
Yin, 38, took on the chairmanship of Chongqing Lifan Technology, a local motorcycle and carmaker, in late 2024, following a bankruptcy restructuring that brought backing from Chongqing municipal government and auto group Geely as key shareholders.
As the auto giant Geely sought to strengthen its intelligent-driving capabilities, Yin was brought in as a shareholder and gradually came to manage the company.

Meanwhile, YinŌĆÖs first venture, Megvii, once one of ChinaŌĆÖs most high-profile AI stars during the computer-vision boom, shelved its long-running initial public offering plans after a three-year bumpy journey, withdrawing the application in November 2024.
Lifan rebranded as Qianli Technology in February 2025.
Over the past year, Qianli has announced multiple collaborations with Geely to deploy its intelligent driving systems across the carmakerŌĆÖs models, striking a ŌĆ£technical partnershipŌĆØ with StepFun.
The company filed for a Hong Kong listing in October and in December secured Mercedes-BenzŌĆÖs China subsidiary as a strategic investor.
Alongside YinŌĆÖs appointment, StepFun, dubbed one of ChinaŌĆÖs ŌĆ£AI tigersŌĆØ, said it had closed a Series B+ round, following a several-hundred-million-dollar Series B raise at the end of 2024.
The latest financing was backed by state-linked capital including Shanghai State-owned Capital Leading Fund, Pudong Venture Capital and Xuhui Capital, WuxiŌĆÖs Liangxi district fund and state-owned Xiamen ITG.
Other participants included China Life Private Equity Investment, electronics manufacturer Huaqin, and existing investors such as Tencent Holdings, Qiming Venture Partners and 5Y Capital.
Investment in ChinaŌĆÖs foundation-model sector remains red hot.
Zhipu AI and MiniMax raised HK$4.3 billion (US$552 million) and HK$4.8 billion, respectively, in Hong Kong listings in January, while Moonshot AI, the maker of chatbot Kimi, raised US$500 million in a recent Series C round, pushing post-valuation to about US$4.3 billion.

data.title: FSDC urges Hong Kong to focus on Africa-Asia financial corridor
data.description:
Hong Kong should focus on connecting its capital markets with African financial centres so that companies and Belt and Road Initiative projects on the continent can raise funds in the city, according to a council that advises the government on development strategy.
In addition to being a funding source for commercial enterprises and infrastructure projects, Hong Kong could be a place for wealthy Africans to set up family offices to manage their wealth, succession and charitable activities, the Financial Services Development Council (FSDC) said in a report on Tuesday. Since 2023, the government has introduced a wide range of measures, including tax incentives and an investment migration scheme, to attract family offices.
ŌĆ£For decades, our city has served as the gateway to China and a critical nexus for AsiaŌĆÖs economic miracle,ŌĆØ said King Au, executive director of the FSDC. ŌĆ£Today, we witness the dawn of another transformative epoch: the rapid acceleration of Asia-Africa economic integration. This is not a transient trend, but the foundation of a long-term partnership that will redefine global growth in the 21st century.ŌĆØ
Four leading African markets ŌĆō Kenya, Morocco, Nigeria and Rwanda ŌĆō could act as entry points for Asian and Hong Kong investors, as they have stable economic growth and sound financial infrastructures, the report said.
China tripled investments under the Belt and Road Initiative in Africa to US$61.2 billion in 2025, fuelled by energy and mining projects as well as a push by manufacturers to take advantage of lower tariff regimes amid the US trade war, according to the Australia-based Griffith Asia Institute.
The initiative, spearheaded by the Chinese government and endorsed by President Xi Jinping in late 2013, aims to improve trade and economic integration via large investments in infrastructure across Asia, Europe and Africa.
Hong Kong-listed Zijin Mining spent US$1 billion to acquire Akyem gold mine, one of the largest in Ghana, in October 2024.
Standard Chartered and other Hong Kong lenders are also active in Africa, providing financing to support expanded trade flows between African and Asian companies.
ŌĆ£As global supply chains recalibrate and new growth poles emerge, the Africa-Asia corridor offers a pathway to diversified, resilient and inclusive economic development for both regions,ŌĆØ the report said. ŌĆ£Hong KongŌĆÖs interest in this dynamic is both strategic and intrinsic.ŌĆØ
Mainland exports to Africa jumped 25.8 per cent to US$225 billion in 2025, far outpacing the 5.4 per cent growth in African exports to China, which reached US$123 billion, according to data from ChinaŌĆÖs General Administration of Customs.
China has been deepening trade with Africa to counter escalating trade tensions with the US following President Donald TrumpŌĆÖs decision to impose higher tariffs on imports of Chinese goods.

Beijing last year introduced a zero-tariff policy that grants duty-free access to its markets for all products from 53 African countries that have diplomatic ties with China.
Africa also wants to improve trade flows. More than 44 markets joined the African Continental Free Trade Area in 2019, creating a unified trading bloc with 1.2 billion consumers. The agreement is part of Agenda 2063, the African UnionŌĆÖs blueprint for transforming the continent into a global powerhouse.
ŌĆ£By aligning standards and facilitating seamless capital flows between African markets and strategic Asian hubs such as Hong Kong, we are turning AfricaŌĆÖs immense potential into tangible, global impact for generations to come,ŌĆØ said Lamia Merzouki, chair of the World Alliance of International Financial Centres, a Brussels-based non-profit association that promotes cooperation among financial centres.

data.title: TencentŌĆÖs Pony Ma teases new AI social feature for Yuanbao app
data.description:
Pony Ma Huateng, founder and CEO of Chinese tech giant Tencent Holdings, has teased a new artificial intelligence-powered social feature in the companyŌĆÖs latest push to keep pace in ChinaŌĆÖs intensifying AI race.
In an internal address to staff, Ma said Tencent was beta testing a new social tool inside its AI assistant app Yuanbao, and encouraged employees to try it out and help debug the feature, according to Tencent staff who attended the meeting.
The feature, known in Chinese as Yuanbao Pai, integrates AI with online social groups, allowing users to interact and collaborate with friends ŌĆō TencentŌĆÖs latest experiment as it expands the capabilities of its consumer-facing AI app.
Tencent did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
TencentŌĆÖs move comes as competition among ChinaŌĆÖs Big Tech groups and AI start-ups continues to heat up, with the Shenzhen-based firm still playing catch-up to front runners such as ByteDance and DeepSeek.
ByteDance-owned Doubao remained the mainlandŌĆÖs top consumer AI app in December, with 155 million weekly active users in the second week last month, according to data from QuestMobile.
That figure was nearly double the 81.56 million recorded by DeepSeekŌĆÖs eponymous chatbot, which ranked as the marketŌĆÖs No 2 player over the same period.
TencentŌĆÖs Yuanbao, Ant GroupŌĆÖs Ant A-Fu and Alibaba CloudŌĆÖs Qwen app were ranked No 3, 4 and 5, respectively, according to QuestMobile. Ant Group is the fintech affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the Post.
Ma also defended TencentŌĆÖs measured approach to AI, even as he acknowledged that the emergence of DeepSeek and OpenAIŌĆÖs ChatGPT had reshaped the trajectory of AI development.
ŌĆ£ItŌĆÖs TencentŌĆÖs style to advance steadily and surely,ŌĆØ Ma told staff at the annual gathering.
He pledged to step up the companyŌĆÖs long-term investment, saying Tencent had already made sweeping upgrades to its infrastructure and talent pool in the field.
TencentŌĆÖs renewed push has been underscored by its recent recruitment of former OpenAI researcher Vinces Yao Shunyu, who was appointed to lead the companyŌĆÖs AI infrastructure efforts, including large language model development.
Yao was named chief AI scientist under the CEOŌĆÖs office, reporting directly to Tencent president Martin Lau Chi-ping.
With YaoŌĆÖs appointment, Ma said Tencent had shifted its focus towards hiring young, ŌĆ£AI-nativeŌĆØ talent, with the goal of ŌĆ£rebuilding TencentŌĆÖs AI research and development from the ground upŌĆØ, according to attendees.
On Sunday, Tencent also escalated its consumer AI push by launching a 1 billion yuan (US$137 million) hong bao giveaway campaign through Yuanbao.
Ma said he hoped the initiative could replicate the success of WeChatŌĆÖs red packet feature, which became an overnight sensation during the Spring Festival in 2014.

data.title: Hong Kong model Abby ChoiŌĆÖs murder trial set for September
data.description:
Abby Choi Tin-fungŌĆÖs ex-husband, his brother and father will stand trial for her alleged killing in September, three years after the death of the Hong Kong model.
In a closed-door hearing on Tuesday, Alex Kwong Kong-chi, his elder brother Anthony Kwong Kong-kit and father Kwong Kau appeared before Deputy High Court Judge Brian Keith for the first time.
The pre-trial hearing was held for the judge to give directions to the parties regarding the trial.
According to the judiciaryŌĆÖs website, the trio did not have legal representation at TuesdayŌĆÖs hearing. They will return to court for a second pre-trial hearing on February 27.
The trial is set to begin on September 21 and is expected to last 45 days at the High Court.
ChoiŌĆÖs death shocked the city in February 2023, when her body parts were found in a flat inside a three-storey house in Lung Mei Tsuen, Tai Po.
The defendants have been remanded in custody since their arrests that same month.
The high-profile murder case then entered a lengthy legal process, with multiple adjournments over the years before being transferred to the High Court for trial.
The defendants were previously represented by lawyer Eric So Chi-kit during earlier hearings.
In a separate case in June 2024, Alex Kwong was sentenced to 3┬Į years in jail for stealing HK$6.3 million (US$806,860) in gold and jewellery and fleeing before his theft trial began in 2015.
His mother, Jenny Li Sui-heung, was jailed in October 2025 for 1┬Į years for obstructing a police investigation into Alex Kwong after the modelŌĆÖs murder. She was found to have prevented police from locating her son and persuaded ChoiŌĆÖs mother to stay silent about his whereabouts.

data.title: ŌĆśAll optionsŌĆÖ considered amid feedback from flat owners hit by Tai Po fire: Lee
data.description:
The Hong Kong government is analysing feedback from 90 per cent of property owners affected by the devastating Tai Po fire, with the city leader stressing that ŌĆ£every optionŌĆØ is being considered amid some residentsŌĆÖ demands for in situ redevelopment.
Two months after the fire that claimed 167 lives, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu on Tuesday underlined the importance of announcing a long-term resettlement plan for thousands of displaced residents, but stopped short of providing a timeline.
He spoke to the press shortly after Erick Tsang Kwok-wai stepped down as secretary for constitutional and mainland affairs, citing health issues due to prostate cancer.
Lee reassured the public that investigations by law enforcement agencies and the independent committee looking into the fire were under way at ŌĆ£full speedŌĆØ, reiterating the governmentŌĆÖs commitment to uncovering the truth and identifying responsible parties as soon as possible.
The fire on November 26 engulfed seven of the eight high-rise residential buildings in Wang Fuk Court, displacing nearly 5,000 people, many of whom are currently staying in temporary accommodation arranged by the government.
To gauge residentsŌĆÖ preferences for long-term rehousing solutions, the government previously distributed questionnaires to flat owners. Options included a buyout plan, a flat-for-flat arrangement, and an in situ development plan, which could take up to 10 years to complete.
Lee said ahead of his weekly meeting with top advisers on Tuesday that the government was analysing feedback from 90 per cent of the flat owners, while housing authorities were reviewing existing projects to provide more options.
ŌĆ£We are expediting all work while taking compassion, reason and law into account,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£We have to make the decision as soon as possible. The sooner the affected families can be rehoused, the better.ŌĆØ
Lee described the long-term rehousing situation as ŌĆ£complicatedŌĆØ, given the diverse circumstances and needs of displaced residents.

Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun, who leads a government task force on emergency resettlement for the residents, earlier shifted his stance on in situ redevelopment. The option gained support among some residents, who voiced their preference in public petitions.
Wong initially described in situ redevelopment as ŌĆ£not practicalŌĆØ. He later acknowledged residentsŌĆÖ wishes but also stressed the significant challenges involved.
While Lee did not provide a specific timeline for the announcement of the resettlement plan, he stressed that ŌĆ£every optionŌĆØ was being weighed. He said the government must also carefully consider the use of public resources, legal issues and property rights.
He added that authorities would ŌĆ£pursue accountability to the very endŌĆØ, ensuring that all responsible parties, whether they were government or private entities, would be held accountable.

data.title: Chinese tech giants Tencent and Baidu join fight for Spring Festival spotlightThe two companies launch digital red packet campaigns following tie-up moves by ByteDance and Alibaba with CCTVŌĆÖs Spring F
data.description:
ChinaŌĆÖs Spring Festival has emerged as a high-stakes arena for artificial intelligence and robotics companies, with tech giants Tencent Holdings and Baidu committing a combined 1.5 billion yuan (US$215 million) to their digital red packet campaigns.
Chinese social media and gaming giant Tencent announced on Sunday that its AI chatbot app Yuanbao would give away 1 billion yuan in hong bao ŌĆō the traditional red envelopes containing cash gifts. Users could win up to 10,000 yuan through lucky draws and gamified tasks on the app, with the campaign set to begin on February 1.
Tencent founder and CEO Pony Ma Huateng told staff at the firmŌĆÖs annual gala on Monday that he hoped the initiative could replicate the success of WeChatŌĆÖs red packet feature.
Search engine and AI giant Baidu initiated its own hong bao campaign on the same day on its app and AI assistant Wenxin, offering 500 million yuan. Users could collect virtual cards and interact with the AI feature to win up to 10,000 yuan.
The moves followed Chinese tech giant ByteDanceŌĆÖs cloud unit Volcano Engine securing an ŌĆ£exclusive AI cloud partnershipŌĆØ with the Spring Festival Gala, mainland ChinaŌĆÖs most-watched annual television event.

Tencent and Alibaba Group Holding had also held talks with state broadcaster China Central Television (CCTV), which produces the show, over partnership packages, the Post reported last month. Alibaba secured the sponsorship in 2025. Alibaba owns the Post.
Beijing-based robotics start-up Galbot announced on Sunday that it had been named ŌĆ£the designated embodied AI model robotŌĆØ for the 2026 gala. The company pledged to showcase ŌĆ£autonomous decision-making capabilities in complex scenarios, high-efficiency collaboration and warm serviceŌĆØ on stage.
The Spring Festival holiday, particularly the CCTV show, has become one of the most important promotional platforms for tech giants as competition intensifies in AI and robotics.
It was a rare opportunity for ŌĆ£national-level trafficŌĆØ, allowing tech products to reach beyond early adopters to the general public, said Zhang Yi, CEO at Shenzhen-based iiMedia Research.
The subsidies by the AI giants would significantly drive user growth and intensify marketing competition in the short term, Zhang said. ŌĆ£However, in the long run, it will remain difficult to break the current hierarchy.ŌĆØ
Tencent has a track record of successful Spring Festival campaigns. In 2014 its mobile wallet WeChat Pay launched digital red packets, which became an overnight sensation with more than 8 million users receiving over 40 million hong bao. In 2015 Tencent partnered with CCTV to promote the feature at the gala, distributing 500 million yuan in prize money.
First broadcast in 1983, the Spring Festival Gala has established itself as a cultural fixture in China. Sponsorship is widely seen as a barometer of corporate strength and political alignment, often signalling blessings from Beijing.
Between 2016 and 2018, Alibaba-affiliated platforms, including mobile payment service Alipay and shopping platform Taobao, held sponsorship rights. The role was later taken up by other tech giants such as Baidu, Kuaishou Technology, ByteDanceŌĆÖs Douyin, and JD.com.
Additional reporting by Eunice Xu

data.title: Hong Kong leader urges patience amid pushback against mandatory seat belt rule
data.description:
Hong KongŌĆÖs leader has called for patience following public complaints about the cityŌĆÖs new mandatory seat belt law, with passengers lamenting that the restraints were poorly fitted for young children and prevented commuters from reaching the ŌĆ£stopŌĆØ buttons on buses to alight.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu on Tuesday said the new regulation was a necessary responsibility that the city must accept to protect lives, and that it was informed by the ŌĆ£painful lessonŌĆØ of the 2018 Tai Po Road bus crash that claimed 19 lives.
ŌĆ£We must all recognise the facts and accept the responsibility of wearing seat belts on public transport,ŌĆØ Lee said ahead of his weekly Executive Council meeting, noting that similar laws had been implemented in mainland China, the United Kingdom, Australia and Singapore.
Singapore does not mandate seat belts on public buses but does so for smaller private-hire buses.
Under Hong KongŌĆÖs new rule, all passengers travelling on public or private buses ŌĆō including franchised and school services ŌĆō must wear a seat belt whenever one is available.
The mandate also applies to the rear seats of private light buses and goods vehicles, as well as to all seats in special-purpose vehicles such as mobile cranes or street-cleaning trucks, provided they were first registered on or after January 25.
Those found violating the law face a maximum fine of HK$5,000 (US$640) and up to three monthsŌĆÖ imprisonment ŌĆō a measure authorities hope will significantly reduce the risk of serious injury in traffic accidents.
Some residents took to social media to express frustration, sharing photos of their childrenŌĆÖs necks being strained or ŌĆ£chokedŌĆØ once buckled up.
ŌĆ£Bus seat belts end up right against a childŌĆÖs neck ŌĆō is this actually safe? When my kid falls asleep, the seat belt just throttles their neck. I have to keep pulling it away with my hand so they can sleep without being choked. My arm is so tired,ŌĆØ one user said.
ŌĆ£Making kids wear seat belts on the bus isnŌĆÖt protecting them at all. It just stays clamped around their neck the whole trip, scratching them. Every time the bus brakes, IŌĆÖm terrified theyŌĆÖll get hurt,ŌĆØ another parent commented.
ŌĆ£Can you please come up with a proper design and think things through before forcing people to follow them?ŌĆØ
Some commuters reported that many seat belts were stiff and difficult to pull, with one saying that he risked touching the adjacent passengerŌĆÖs thigh.
ŌĆ£Can anyone teach me how to press the ŌĆśstopŌĆÖ bell without safely unbuckling? Even with arms as long as mine, I still canŌĆÖt reach the button [at the top],ŌĆØ another user lamented.
Lee addressed these concerns by acknowledging that new measures often bring initial ŌĆ£inconvenienceŌĆØ, but he insisted the safety benefits ultimately outweighed the challenges of changing oneŌĆÖs habits.

He cited research showing that in head-on collisions, wearing a seat belt could reduce the risk of serious injury by 70 per cent and the risk of fatality by 40 per cent.
Lee emphasised that the mandate was not arbitrary, but a direct response to the tragic 2018 Tai Po Road bus crash that left 19 dead and 66 injured. The policy was a core recommendation from the independent review committee and experts tasked with preventing similar accidents resulting in mass casualties.
ŌĆ£This is a measure to ensure passenger safety, proposed following research, discussion and a painful lesson,ŌĆØ Lee said.
Acknowledging the friction caused by the new rules, he added: ŌĆ£I understand that new measures will bring about changes in behaviour, and it will be inconvenient at first while people get used to them.ŌĆØ
The city leader noted that the initial stage of implementation would focus on public education and ŌĆ£fostering habits through persuasionŌĆØ.
Regarding complaints on the quality of seat belts, Lee said he had instructed the Transport and Logistics Bureau to issue clear requirements to operators to properly manage the cleanliness, performance and installation of seat belts.
ŌĆ£We also hope that through explanation, everyone will understand that the law allows for ŌĆśreasonable excusesŌĆÖ in certain emergency or sudden situations, where one would not be held liable,ŌĆØ Lee said.
ŌĆ£In handling these various matters, we will proceed with a balance of law, reason and compassion.ŌĆØ

data.title: BlackpinkŌĆÖs Ros├® delights Hong Kong fans with local snack choices during shows
data.description:
Egg waffles, Hong Kong-style milk tea and curry fish balls made surprise appearances at BlackpinkŌĆÖs concerts in the city, delighting fans and residents with the K-pop starŌĆÖs embrace of local culture.
Over three nights of BlackpinkŌĆÖs final world tour shows at the 50,000-seat Kai Tak Stadium this past weekend, concertgoers eagerly speculated about what the bandŌĆÖs lead singer, Ros├®, would eat on her way to the stage before performing her solo segment.
ŌĆ£[IŌĆÖm] dying with laughter. Everyone was guessing what Ros├® would eat, and I heard someone behind me say ŌĆśtwo-dish riceŌĆÖ,ŌĆØ social media user ŌĆśyamefungŌĆÖ posted.
The singerŌĆÖs choice of local snack became a highlight of the groupŌĆÖs brief stay in Hong Kong, with residents making guesses ahead of each concert.
Classic Hong Kong favourites such as egg tarts, pineapple buns and siu mai dominated online discussions, while others suggested viral hits like the meat patty from ŌĆ£bing suttŌĆØ caf├® Tai Bo in Yuen Long.
The frenzy formed part of Ros├®ŌĆÖs ongoing repertoire throughout the ŌĆ£DeadlineŌĆØ world tour that began in July last year. While most touring artists typically learn basic greetings in the local language, she chose to sample popular street foods alongside her cheerful ŌĆ£dai gaa hoŌĆØ [ŌĆ£hello everyoneŌĆØ] greetings.
Her culinary curiosity also served as an inadvertent promotion for shops selling the snacks sampled. Traditional bakery Jiu Zhen Nan, whose Taiwanese pineapple pastry Ros├® tried during her Kaohsiung concert in October, thanked the artist publicly for trying their product.
Taiwanese social media users, meanwhile, were quick to speculate which drink from the classic bubble tea brand 50Lan she had tried backstage.

In Hong Kong, the singer began her run of shows with a bite of an egg waffle on Saturday, before moving on to milk tea served in a signature black and white milk ceramic cup commonly found in cha chaan tengs. She concluded her performances on Monday by sampling curry fish balls.
But social media users in the city jokingly guessed that Ros├®ŌĆÖs team had sourced snacks from the nearest street stall to the stadium rather than from any particularly famous eatery.
A source familiar with the matter told the Post that she had not partnered with any official organisations in Hong Kong when deciding which treats to sample.
Aside from Ros├®, her bandmate Lisa was also spotted visiting the Che Kung Temple in Sha Tin on Sunday. Dressed in a pink hoodie and glasses, the Thai star kept a low profile as she was seen holding incense sticks and a pack of joss paper in a devout gesture.
Interest in BlackpinkŌĆÖs movements after their concerts remained intense, with internet users tracking where they visited and the food they enjoyed.
Jennie, who turned 30 on January 16, while Blackpink were performing in Tokyo, was revealed to have hosted a birthday celebration at the 1 OAK club in Azabu-Juban after videos of her dancing at the venue surfaced online.

data.title: Hong Kong police probe pond cleaning operation that killed more than 100 fish
data.description:
Hong Kong police have launched an investigation into animal cruelty after more than 100 fish, including large koi, died while a pond was being cleaned at a public housing estate in Tseung Kwan O.
The incident occurred on Monday morning when a cleaning contractor removed the fish to facilitate maintenance, but allegedly failed to provide a temporary space with adequate aeration, according to the animal rights group Hong Kong Pigeon and Dove Rescue.
ŌĆ£Someone has called for help. A cleaning company came to wash the fish pond and simply scooped all the fish out and set them aside ŌĆ” Since 1pm [on Monday], the death toll has been rising continuously because there are no air pumps,ŌĆØ the group wrote in a Facebook post.
The force on Tuesday said it received a report at 8.01pm on Monday from a passer-by who discovered dozens of fish kept in three separate crates outside a pond near King Min House at King Lam Estate.
Initially classified as a request for police assistance, the case was subsequently reclassified as cruelty to animals. A probe is underway by the Tseung Kwan O district criminal investigation team, although no arrests had been made as of Tuesday morning.

The aftermath of the mass die-off was captured in a widely circulated photograph showing dozens of dead fish, including large orange-and-white koi, laid out in neat rows across the wet brick pavement.
The rescue group provided updates throughout the night. By 3.30am on Tuesday, it confirmed the death toll had reached 104.
A volunteer rescue effort involving the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals and the conservation group TurtleŌĆÖs Inn managed to stabilise the surviving fish and relocate them to nearby ponds, the group added.
Under the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Ordinance, those found responsible for causing unnecessary suffering to animals face a maximum penalty of three years in prison and a fine of HK$200,000 (US$25,600).
